analogy: Think of "International Organisations" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview This chapter examines the growing significance of environmental as well as resource issues in world politics. It analyses in a comparative perspective some of the important environmental movements against the backdrop of the rising profile of environmentalism from the 1960s onwar ds. Notions... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
International Organisations is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o verview This chapter examines the growing significance of environmental as well as resource issues in world politics.

The text explains that it analyses in a comparative perspective some of the important environmental movements against the backdrop of the rising profile of environmentalism from the 1960s onwar ds.

The text explains that notions of common property resources and the global commons too are assessed.

The text explains that we also discuss, in brief, the stand taken by India in more recent environmental debates.

The text explains that next follows a brief account of the geopolitics of resource competition.

The text explains that we conclude by taking note of the indigenous peoples’ voices and concerns from the margins of contemporary world politics.

The text explains that chapter 6 Environment and Natural Resources The 1992 Earth Summit has brought environmental issues to the centre-stage of global politics.

The text explains that the pictures above show rainforest and mangroves.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 82 E nvironmEntal ConCErns in Global PolitiCs In this book we have discussed ‘world politics’ in a fairly limited sense: wars and treaties, rise and decline of state power, the relationship between the governments that represent their countries in the international arena and the role of inter- governmental organisations.

The text explains that in Chapter 5, we expanded the scope of world politics to include issues like poverty and epidemics.

The text explains that that may not have been a very difficult step to take, for we all think that governments are responsible for controlling these.

The text explains that in that sense they fall within the scope of world politics.

The text explains that do you think they fall within the scope of contemporary world politics?

The text explains that t h r o u g h o u t t h e w o r l d , cultivable area is barely expanding any more, and a substantial portion of existing agricultural land is losing fertility.

The text explains that grasslands have been overgrazed and fisheries over- harvested.

The text explains that water bodies have suffered extensive depletion a n d p o l l u t i o n , s e v e r e l y restricting food production.

The text explains that 83 Environment and N atural R esources becoming increasingly polluted largely due to land-based activities.

The text explains that issues of environment and natural resources are political in another deeper sense.

The text explains that who gets to use how much of the natural resources of the Earth?

The text explains that the Club of Rome, a global think tank, published a book in 1972 entitled Limits to Growth, dramatising the potential depletion of the Earth’s resources against the backdrop of rapidly growing world population.

The text explains that let us look at some of the contentious issues in the global politics of environment. thE ProtECtion of Global Commons ‘Commons’ are those resources which are not owned by anyone but rather shared by a community.

The text explains that 85 Environment and N atural R esources areas or regions of the world which are located outside the sovereign jurisdiction of any one state, and therefore require common governance by the international community.

The text explains that the Antarctic plays an important role in maintaining climatic equilibrium, and deep ice cores provide an important source of information about greenhouse gas concentrations and atmospheric temperatures of hundreds and thousands of years ago.

The text explains that source: www.gobartimes.org Find out more about the Kyoto Protocol.

The text explains that 87 Environment and N atural R esources and the South.

The text explains that the developed c o u n t r i e s a c k n o w l e d g e t h e responsibility that they bear in the international pursuit of sustainable development in view of the pressures their societies place on the global environment and of the technological and financial resources they command.” The 1992 United Nations F r a m e w o r k C o n v e n t i o n o n Climate Change (UNFCCC) also provides that the parties should act to protect the climate system “on the basis of equity and in accordance with their common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities.” The parties to the Convention agreed that the largest share of historical and current global emissions of greenhouse gases has originated in developed countries.

The text explains that certain gases like Carbon dioxide, Methane, Hydro- fluoro carbons etc. are considered at least partly responsible for global warming - the rise in global temperature which may have catastrophic consequences for life on Earth.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 88 Common ProPErty rEsourCEs Common property represents common property for the group.

The text explains that the underlying norm here is that members of the group have both rights and duties with respect to the nature, levels of use, and the maintenance of a given resource.

The text explains that as a model of community-based resource management, groves have lately gained attention in conservation literature.

The text explains that the sacred groves can be seen as a system that informally forces traditional communities to harvest natural resources in an ecologically sustained fashion.

The text explains that sacred groves embody a rich set of forest preservation practices and they share characteristics with common property resource systems.

The text explains that deep religious reverence for nature, rather than resource scarcity, seems to be the basis for the long-standing commitment to preserving these forests.

The text explains that 89 Environment and N atural R esources emission of greenhouse gases during the industrialisation period (that is believed to be causing today’s global warming and climate change) was not significant.

The text explains that one of the key conclusions was that there had been no meaningful progress with respect to transfer of new and additional financial resources and envir onmentally-sound technology on concessional terms to developing nations.

The text explains that india finds it necessary that developed c o u n t r i e s t a k e i m m e d i a t e measures to provide developing countries with financial resources and clean technologies to enable them to meet their existing commitments under UNFCCC.

The text explains that 91 Environment and N atural R esources movements raise new ideas and long-term visions of what we should do and what we should not do in our individual and collective lives.

The text explains that the forest movements of the South, in Mexico, Chile, Brazil, Malaysia, Indonesia, continental Africa and India (just to list a few examples) are faced with enormous pressures.

The text explains that the minerals industry is one of the most powerful forms of industry on the planet.

The text explains that the mineral industry’s extraction of earth, its use of chemicals, i t s p o l l u t i o n o f waterways and land, its clearance of native vegetation, its displacement of communities, amongst other factors, continue to invite criticism and resistance in various parts of the globe.

The text explains that it is significant to note that, in anti-dam and other environmental movements in India, the most important shared idea is non-violence. rEsourCE GEoPolitiCs R e s o u r c e g e o p o l i t i c s i s a l l about who gets what, when, where and how.

The text explains that resources have provided some of the key means and motives of global European power expansion.

The text explains that western geopolitical thinking about resources has been dominated by the relationship of trade, war and power, at the core of which were overseas resources and maritime navigation.

The text explains that the critical importance of ensuring uninterrupted supply of strategic resources, in particular oil, was well established both during the First World War and the Second World War.

The text explains that throughout the Cold War the industrialised countries of the North adopted a number of methods to ensure a steady flow of resources.

The text explains that 93 Environment and N atural R esources deployment of military forces near exploitation sites and along sea-lanes of communication, the stockpiling of strategic resources, e f f o r t s t o p r o p u p f r i e n d l y g o v e r n m e n t s i n p r o d u c i n g countries, as well as support to multinational companies and favourable international agreements.

The text explains that a particular concern was Western control of oil in the Gulf and strategic minerals in Southern and Central Africa.

The text explains that after the end of the Cold War and the disintegration of the Soviet Union, the security of supply continues to worry government and business decisions with regard to several minerals, in particular radioactive materials.

The text explains that which among the following are TRUE about the global commons? a.

The text explains that the Earth’s atmosphere, Antarctica, ocean floor and outer space are considered as part of the global commons. b.

The text explains that the global commons are outside sovereign jurisdiction. c.

The text explains that the question of managing the global commons has reflected the North-South divide. d.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 6: International Organisations

prelims_traps: Do not treat 83 Environment and N atural R esources and 85 Environment and N atural R esources as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to International Organisations; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain International Organisations through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 83 Environment and N atural R esources and 85 Environment and N atural R esources to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
